title: Vibhuti Pada
summary: Focuses on concentration (dharana), meditation (dhyana), absorption (samadhi), and spiritual powers. Addresses deep focus and intuitive insights.

sections.summary: This passage begins with “Concentration/Attention is focusing on a single place.” and develops toward “However, this insight does not extend to the object (alambana*) of their perceptions since it (the object) is not actually the subject matter of the…”

This passage begins with “By observing the body's physical form with perfect discipline (samyama*), it becomes invisible. This happens when the power to perceive is suspended amidst the eye and the incoming light.” and develops toward “By acheiving mastery over the flow of energy through the solar plexus (samana*), one becomes radiant.”

This passage begins with “By intently focusing (samyama*) on the inter-relationship of the organ of hearing and space, one can attain divine hearing.” and develops toward “The state of 'kaivalya' or liberation dawns when the purity of intellect equals to that of the pure consciousness.”

verses.translation: Concentration/Attention is focusing on a single place.
In meditation, one's entire perceptual experience becomes uninturreptedly aligned with that object.
When only the essence of that object (used for dhyana) remains, and (chitta's) own functions (other cognitive abilities) appears non-existent then samadhi* has been achieved.
The pure state of cognition (samyama*) involves all the (above) three (concentration, meditative absorption, and integration towards a single object.)
When one masters this discipline, light of wisdom dawns. [Stages of this wisdom has been mentioned in sutra 2.27]
It (Samyama* or pure state of cognition) should be applied (sequentially) on various stages or planes (of refined cognition).
These three limbs (concentration, absorption, and integration) are innermost with respect to the previous five.
However, even these three are considered extrinsic with respect to the state of integration that bears no potential/seeds (Nirbeej Samadhi*).

The cyclical suppression of worldly or distracting smaskaras and emergence of restraining (nirodha*) samskaras eventually fades the former resulting in the frequent contact of the chitta with restraining samskaras. This enables the state of restraint or Nirodha-parinama*.
The unhinged fludity of the thoughts in the mind is due to the samskaras.
In the state of liberation (Samadhi parinama*), the chitta eliminates multi-pointedness and develops one-pointedness.
Then, again, the one object or idea of focus present/arising on the chitta is identical to the one which subsides. This state is of one-pointedness (Ekagrata parinama*).

Similar to this (like the above changes in consiousness), dynamic changes (due to interplay of gunas) in material objects and senses can be interpreted at three levels : funtion (dharma*), timespan (lakshana*) and condition (awastha*).
The underlying foundation (dharmi*) remains the same, irrespective of whether it manifests in a particular form or funtion (dharma*) - before, during, or after the transformation.
These transformations (of objects/awareness) seem to occur in a specific manner, which is caused by change in sequence (of characteristics).
By carefully observing (a pure state of cognition or samyama*) the three aspects of change - function, timespan, and condition, one can gain insights into both the past and future.

The concepts of word, meaning, and perception are often intertwined, creating confusion between them. However, by paying attention to the differences between them through perfect discipline (samyama*), one can gain insights into the sounds of all living beings.
Examining the dormant mental impressions (samskaras*) through direct perception bestows insights into past lives.
It is possible to gain insight into the consciousness of another person by focusing on their perceptions (pratyaya*).
However, this insight does not extend to the object (alambana*) of their perceptions since it (the object) is not actually the subject matter of the one who is focussing (i.e. the yogi).
By observing the body's physical form with perfect discipline (samyama*), it becomes invisible. This happens when the power to perceive is suspended amidst the eye and the incoming light.

verses.sanskrit: सोपक्रमं निरुपक्रमं च कर्म तत्संयमादपरान्तज्ञानमरिष्टेभ्यो वा
verses.translation: The consequences of actions (karma) may manifest instantly or take time to appear. Observing one's actions with perfect discipline or studying omens can offer insight into death.
By using perfect discipline to focus on friendliness etc.(i.e compassion, delight, and equanimity), one can internalize (their) strengths.
Through focusing (samyama*) on strengths, it is possible to acquire the powers of an elephant, etc.
Being deeply engaged in the radiance of the mind provides insight into the subtle, concealed, and remote aspects of existence (of things).
With perfect focusing (samayama*) on the sun, allows one to gain insight into the universe.
By using perfect discipline (samayama*) to focus on the moon, one can gain insight into the position of the stars.
Perfect discipline (samayama*) enables one to gain insight into the movements/speed of the stars by focusing on the polestar.
By using perfect discipline (samayama*) to focus on the navel plexus, one can gain insight into the organization of the body.
Focusing with perfect discipline (samayama*) on the pit of the throat helps to eliminate hunger and thirst.
Focusing with perfect discipline (samayama*) on the 'tortoise channel' (kurma naadi) helps to cultivate steadiness.
Through perfect discipline (samayama*), focusing on the light at the crown of the head allows one to acquire the perspective of those who have achieved perfection (siddhas).
Or, intuition (a foreground of districrinatory knowledge) may bring about knowlede of all.
By concentrating with utmost discipline on the heart, one can comprehend the essence of consciousness.

Wordly perception comprises of experiences where the pure consiousness (purusha*) is misconceived as pure (sattva*) intellect, whereas both are seperate. However, wordly perception exists to serve the comprehension of the other (i.e. pure consiousness). By concentrating with utmost discipline on the one that dwells for itself, understanding of the pure consciousness is revealed.
As a result of this (above) understanding, intuition and elevated sense of hearing, touch, sight, taste, smell emerge.
Although these heightened sensory abilities may seem like accomplishments to outwardly directed mind, they are obstacles in the path of liberation/integration.
By letting go of one's attachment(s) and being acutely attuned to the pathways of consciousness, it can settle in another individual's body.

By achieving mastery over the vital energy in the neck and head (udana*), one can traverse water, mud, thorns, and other obstacles without making contact with the ground, but instead floating over them.
By acheiving mastery over the flow of energy through the solar plexus (samana*), one becomes radiant.
By intently focusing (samyama*) on the inter-relationship of the organ of hearing and space, one can attain divine hearing.
By intently focusing (samyama*) on the body's connection with the space and cultivating intense concentration (samapatti*) on the lightness as of cotton, one can travel through space.
The state of 'great disembodiment' (mahavidheha*) happens when the mind is projected outside the body into a non-imagined state. Then the veil obscuring the mind's radiance is lifted.

By intently observing the different dimensions of matter such as gross, intrinsic, subtle, interconnectedness (at the level of gunas) and purpose (of objects) with samyama*, one attains mastery over the elements.

As a result, extraordinary abilities, such as the power to reduce oneself to the size of an atom (anima*) etc. ( and 7 more abilities) manifest. Thus the body develops ability to transcend the laws of the physical matter and attains perfection too.
This level of perfection of the body includes qualities such as beauty, grace, strength, and the indestructibility of a thunderbolt.

One can gain control over sense organs by samyama* (on the various aspects of sense organs) - the process of obtaining knowledge, basic properties (of the sense organs), the individuating principle (asmita*), the inherent qualities and need (of the gunas*).
By doing that (samyama over sense organs) one also accquires ability to travel with the speed of thought, body independant perception and control over fundamental facets of matter (mula prakriti*).
Only when an individual perceives the distinction between pure awareness and the the intellect, they become knower of all and gain authority over existence.
When one detaches even from this omniscience and mastery, and the seeds of suffering decay, and pristine consciousness realizes its independent existence (state of kaivalya*).
Even if the divine beings call upon one, it is vital to refrain from becoming attached or arrogant; otherwise, suffering will resurface.
By intently focusing (samyama*) on the succession of moments in time, one gains insights that arise from discernment.
Due to which, distinction even between seemingly similar things on the basis of species, properties and location can be ascertained.

verses.sanskrit: तारकं सर्वविषयं सर्वथाविषयमक्रमं चेति विवेकजं ज्ञानम्
verses.translation: This liberating insight deconstructs each object and every conditions in an time independant manner (i.e it is never changing unlike any other knowledge) and signifies viveka jnanam* or liberative discernment.
The state of 'kaivalya' or liberation dawns when the purity of intellect equals to that of the pure consciousness.
